Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 1 - The Sexual Offences Framework
The sexual offences framework in Irish law is layered. The principal modern statute is the Criminal Law (Sexual
Offences) Act 2017, which substantially reformed consent doctrine and child sexual offences. But several older
statutes remain in force (notably the Criminal Law (Rape) Act 1981 defining rape, and the Criminal Law
(Rape)(Amendment) Act 1990 defining s.4 rape and sexual assault). This chapter sets out the architecture.

1.1 The Principal Statutes - The Architecture

1.2 Consent - The 2017 Act Definition
s.48 Criminal Law (Sexual Offences) Act 2017

1.2.1 The Three Core Attributes
The 2017 Act consent doctrine has three core attributes:
Active - consent must be given, not assumed from absence of refusal. Silence, passivity, or failure to resist is
not consent.
Voluntary - consent must be freely given. Consent obtained by force, threat, fraud, deception, or impairment is
not consent.
Ongoing - consent applies to the specific act at the specific time. Consent to one act is not consent to another.
Consent can be withdrawn at any point.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

1.2.2 The Statutory Indicators of Lack of Consent - s.48
s.48 specifies circumstances in which a person does not consent to a sexual act:
Force / threat of force against the person or another.
Unlawfully detained.
Asleep or unconscious.
Incapable of consenting because of intoxication.
Mistaken about identity of the person engaging in the act.
Mistaken about the nature of the act.
Submission as a result of words or conduct that exploits a position of dependence, trust, or authority.
Submission as a result of words or conduct from the other person.
Physically incapable of communicating consent.

1.2.3 Capacity to Consent
Beyond the statutory indicators, capacity to consent is a separate doctrine:
Intoxication - a person too intoxicated to understand or evaluate the situation cannot consent. The threshold is
not 'any alcohol' but the level at which the person lacks capacity.
Age - persons under the age of consent (currently 17 in Irish law) cannot legally consent to sexual activity,
regardless of any apparent agreement. The under-17 offences (s.16, s.17 CL(SO)A 2017) apply regardless of
apparent consent.
Mental capacity - persons with cognitive / intellectual disability may lack capacity. The protection of vulnerable
persons regime (s.21 CL(SO)A 2017) addresses sexual offences against persons unable to consent due to mental
impairment.

1.2.4 Withdrawal of Consent
Consent can be withdrawn at any time. Continuation of sexual activity after withdrawal of consent is nonconsensual. This is operationally important - victims may have consented to initial activity but not to what
followed; the prosecution case can be built on the post-withdrawal acts.

1.3 The Categories of Sexual Offences
The framework organises offences by victim category and nature of conduct:

1.3.1 Penile-Penetrative Offences
Rape (s.2 CL(R)A 1981) - vagina by penis.
Section 4 rape (s.4 CL(R)(A)A 1990) - anus or mouth by penis; vagina by object.
Both carry life imprisonment maximum.

1.3.2 Non-Penetrative Sexual Offences
Sexual assault (s.2 CL(R)(A)A 1990) - non-penetrative sexual touching without consent. (Formerly indecent
assault.)
Aggravated sexual assault (s.3 CL(R)(A)A 1990) - sexual assault involving serious violence or threat thereof;
or gross indignity.

1.3.3 Sexual Offences Against Children
Defilement of child under 17 (s.17 CL(SO)A 2006 / s.18 CL(SO)A 2017 reformed framework).
Defilement of child under 15 (s.16 CL(SO)A 2006 framework).
Sexual offences against children under 13 - aggravated provisions.
Online grooming (s.8 CL(SO)A 2017).
Causing a child to watch or engage in sexual activity (CL(SO)A 2017).

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

1.3.4 Specialist / Aggravated Categories
Incest (CL(SO)(A)A 2007).
Sexual offences against vulnerable persons (s.21 CL(SO)A 2017).
Sexual exploitation - sex trafficking (CL(HT)A 2008).
Child sexual exploitation material - production, distribution, possession (CTPA 1998 framework).
Image-based sexual abuse (HHCROA 2020 - Coco's Law).
Sexual harassment in workplace / public (employment / equality law framework + criminal where threshold
met).

1.4 Anonymity Provisions
Sexual offences cases attract statutory anonymity protections:
Complainant anonymity - s.7 Criminal Law (Rape) Act 1981 (as amended) - identifying information of
complainant cannot be published. Breach is an offence.
Accused anonymity in certain cases - in some sexual offence proceedings the accused is also entitled to
anonymity until conviction (subject to specific statutory provisions).
Internal information handling - the protections apply internally as well. Investigation discussion should be
confined to operational need-to-know.

1.5 The Sex Offender Register
The Sex Offenders Act 2001 established the registration regime. Persons convicted of specified sexual offences
are subject to notification requirements - registration with Gardai, address details, travel notifications. The
framework operates as a post-conviction risk management tool. Garda Sex Offender Management Units
coordinate. Specific provisions on registration scope and duration are in the 2001 Act and subsequent
amendments. (Detailed treatment in Chapter 7.)

1.6 The Children First Interface
Children First Act 2015 mandatory reporting applies to sexual offences against children. Gardai (and other
Mandated Persons) report concerns to Tusla. The Tusla / criminal investigation interface:
Children First report - separate from criminal report; engages Tusla welfare framework.
Garda investigation continues in parallel with Tusla welfare process.
Joint working protocols - structured interface between Tusla social work and Garda investigation.
Children First Officer at District level coordinates.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 2 - Rape
Rape is the gravest sexual offence in Irish law. The framework comprises two principal provisions: s.2 of the
Criminal Law (Rape) Act 1981 (rape - penile penetration of vagina without consent), and s.4 of the Criminal
Law (Rape)(Amendment) Act 1990 (penetration of anus or mouth by penis, or vagina by object - extends the
rape framework to acts not within the 1981 Act definition). Both carry life imprisonment maximum. The
Criminal Law (Sexual Offences) Act 2017 reformed consent doctrine which now governs the consent element.

2.1 Rape - s.2 Criminal Law (Rape) Act 1981
s.2 CL(R)A 1981 (as amended)

2.1.1 Elements
Sexual intercourse: Penetration (however slight) of the vagina by the penis.
Without consent: The complainant did not consent within the s.48 CL(SO)A 2017 framework.
Knowledge / recklessness as to non-consent: The accused knew the complainant did not consent OR was
reckless as to whether she consented. Recklessness is Murray-subjective: the accused was aware of the risk that
she did not consent and proceeded.
'Unlawful': Historically the marital rape exemption made marital intercourse 'lawful' regardless of consent.
The Criminal Law (Rape)(Amendment) Act 1990 abolished the exemption - rape within marriage is rape.

2.1.2 The 1990 Act Extension
The Criminal Law (Rape)(Amendment) Act 1990 extends rape to include:
Penetration of anus or mouth by penis (s.4).
Penetration of vagina by an object (s.4).
These offences are treated alongside s.2 rape with equivalent penalties (life imprisonment maximum).

2.1.3 Court and Penalty
Court: Central Criminal Court (Circuit Criminal Court only where Director consents).
Penalty: Up to life imprisonment.

2.2 Live Incident Response - Rape
2.2.1 The Initial Disclosure
Rape rarely presents at 'live' incident stage in the sense of other offences. The typical first contact is:
Victim presents at Garda Station to report.
Victim presents at hospital A&E, which contacts Gardai.
Victim discloses to family / friend who contacts Gardai.
Victim presents at SATU, which engages Gardai (if victim consents to report).
Third-party report (occasionally - witness, family member).
Disclosure during DV / other investigation.

2.2.2 First Response Protocol
Private, safe location for initial conversation.
Trauma-informed approach - listen, don't lead, don't interrupt.
Initial account preserved - BWV (with consent / standard practice) or contemporaneous notes.
Belief - the operational posture is to believe.
Immediate medical needs assessed - injuries, pregnancy / STI considerations - SATU referral.
Forensic preservation - clothing, scene if accessible, evidence preservation advice (do not wash, do not change

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

clothes, preserve sheets/items).
Specialist support - call the Divisional Domestic Abuse and Sexual Crime Investigation Unit (DARI) / Garda
DSCB for specialist engagement.
Victim support - explain options, refer to Rape Crisis Centre, victim services.
Children if involved - Tusla / Children First.
Safety planning - if the suspect is known, accessible, or dangerous.

2.3 Investigation Strategy
2.3.1 The Victim Statement
Specialist Garda Victim Interviewer (where available).
Multi-session approach common - the account develops over engagement.
Comfortable, private location - Garda Specialist Interview Suite where available.
Video / audio recorded statement under CEA 1992 framework (typically for vulnerable witnesses; subject to
consent and procedure).
Allow the victim to set pace, take breaks, choose what to disclose when.
Use of intermediaries for victims with communication needs.
Verbatim recording of disclosure - the victim's own words matter.

2.3.2 SATU Examination
Referral - ideally within 72 hours but later examinations valuable.
Sexual Offences Examination Kit (SOEK) - standardised forensic kit.
Photography of injuries by SATU clinician.
DNA / biological samples - from victim's body, clothing, hair.
Medical care - STI screening, emergency contraception, mental health.
SATU report becomes evidence.
Victim continuity - SATU clinician may need to give evidence at trial.

2.3.3 Scene Investigation
Where the offence occurred at an identifiable scene - secure, preserve, document.
Scene examination by Garda Crime Scene Investigator (CSI).
Biological evidence retrieval - bedding, surfaces, items.
Photography, plans, measurements.
Witness identification at scene context.

2.3.4 Suspect Investigation
Identification - typically known to victim (most rape offenders are known to the victim).
Arrest planning - timing, location, support.
On arrest - caution; secure clothing for forensic examination; transport to custody.
Custody - medical assessment; pre-interview consultation; interview planning.
Suspect SATU-style examination - typically requires consent or court order; biological samples; clothing
forensic; injuries photographed.
Premises search where appropriate (Damache warrant).
Communications evidence - phone, social media.

2.3.5 Communications and Digital
Messages between victim and suspect - before, during, after the incident.
Social media content.
Phone forensic examination.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

CCTV - venue, route, surrounding area.
Vehicle tracking / journey records.

2.3.6 Third-Party Witnesses
Persons the victim told (recent complaint - see Evidence Appendix).
Persons who saw victim or suspect before / after.
Venue staff if relevant.
Healthcare professionals who saw victim.
Friends / family - the victim's network.

2.4 Suspect Strategy and Arrest Power
2.4.1 Specimen Arrest Words - Rape
'[Name], I am arresting you on suspicion of an offence of rape contrary to section 2 of the Criminal Law (Rape)
Act 1981 [or section 4 of the Criminal Law (Rape)(Amendment) Act 1990 as appropriate]. You are alleged to
have had sexual intercourse with [complainant] on [date] at [location] without her consent. You are not obliged
to say anything unless you wish to do so but anything you do say will be taken down in writing and may be
given in evidence.'

2.4.2 Detention Framework
Arrestable offence - s.4 CJA 1984 detention regime.
Up to 24 hours in stages with appropriate authorisations.
Detention extension justification - what investigative steps require further time (forensic examination of
clothing, interview planning, premises search, witness statements).

2.5 Interview Plan - Rape Suspect
Identification.
Account of relationship to complainant - prior contact, current contact.
Account of the relevant period - what the suspect says happened.
Consent analysis - did the suspect believe there was consent, on what basis, was that belief honestly and
reasonably held.
Specific acts - did sexual activity occur, what kind, when, where.
Force / threat / circumstances of submission - put the s.48 indicators to the suspect.
Communications - put extracted messages to suspect.
Complainant's intoxication / vulnerability - did the suspect appreciate.
Post-incident conduct - flight, communications, attempts to influence the complainant.
Anticipate defences: consent (the principal defence); identification (in stranger cases); fabrication (the
credibility attack).
Inferences from silence (s.18, 19, 19A CJA 1984) - Buck-strict compliance.

2.6 PULSE / File Index - Rape
Rape files are major. The structure:

2.7 Charge Selection
Conduct

Charge

Penile penetration of vagina without consent

consent

2.8 Specimen Wording - Garda Statement Excerpt
'On [date] at [time] [complainant] attended at [Station] and made a complaint of rape. I accompanied
[complainant] to [private room] and ensured she was accompanied by [support person]. I took an initial brief
account from [complainant] preserving her own words. She stated that on [date] at [location] [suspect,
identified] [brief account]. I made arrangements for [complainant] to attend SATU [unit] and accompanied her.
Specialist interview by Garda [name] was conducted on [date] and recorded under CEA 1992 procedures.
[Suspect] was arrested by me at [location] on [date] at [time] on suspicion of rape and cautioned. [Suspect's]
clothing was seized and submitted to FSI; biological samples were obtained at custody under appropriate
consent. The forensic, scene, and communications evidence is collated at [file references].'

2.9 Defence Will Attack
Consent - the principal defence. The defence may concede that intercourse occurred and contest consent. Rebut
by: complainant's account; s.48 indicators (force, threat, intoxication, dependence, submission); recent
complaint; injuries; suspect's post-incident conduct; communications evidence.
Honest belief in consent - the defence may concede non-consent but contend the accused honestly and
reasonably believed there was consent. Rebut by: objective indicators that consent was absent and an honest
person would have known; the s.48 indicators that should have alerted; the accused's awareness of intoxication
/ vulnerability / lack of response.
Identification - in stranger cases, the identification of the suspect challenged. Rebut by: DNA, forensic, CCTV,
identification procedure (VIPER), description quality.
Fabrication - the defence may suggest the complaint is fabricated. Rebut by: corroborating evidence (forensic,
witness, contemporaneous communications), absence of identifiable motive to lie, recent complaint at first
opportunity.
Inconsistency - the defence will probe inconsistencies between accounts. Rebut by: trauma-informed expert
evidence (where appropriate); recognising that inconsistency in peripheral detail does not undermine credibility
on central facts.
Procedural challenge - JC pathway on procedural breach. Rebut by procedural rigour at every step.

2.10 Red Flag Escalation
Multiple complainants - same suspect across multiple complainants - investigation widens; pattern evidence
consideration.
Use of weapons / serious violence.
False imprisonment / sustained detention.
Vulnerable complainant - child, person with disability, person under coercive control.
Suspect in position of trust / authority - teacher, healthcare worker, religious figure - aggravating, particular
procedural sensitivity.
Drug-facilitated sexual offence (DFSA) - toxicology examination essential; specific evidential framework.
Group offence / multiple suspects.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Historical disclosure - Volume 6 Chapter 7 framework.

2.11 Common Pitfalls
Inadequate first response - disbelief, leading questions, secondary victimisation.
SATU delay or omission - forensic window slipping.
Evidence handling shortcuts - chain of custody compromised.
Specialist interview not engaged - inexperienced interviewer attempting complex disclosure.
Failure to preserve communications evidence early - victim or suspect deleting messages.
Inadequate engagement with multi-agency support - Rape Crisis Centre, Tusla.
Suspect interview without proper preparation - Gormley compliance, evidence ready, anticipated defences
thought through.

2.12 Ethics Touchpoints
Standard 1 - Article 3 ECHR positive obligation to investigate effectively (M.C. v Bulgaria).
Standard 5 - respect for the complainant throughout.
Standard 6 - impartiality across complainant and suspect demographics.
Anonymity protections - internally and externally.

2.13 Compliment Points / Cross-References
Strong rape investigations are at the top of operational scale - formal commendation worthy.
Cross-reference: Ch.3 (sexual assault); Ch.4 (consent depth); Ch.7 (investigation framework); Volume 2
(NFOAP for ancillary charges); Volume 4 (DV-context sexual offences); Case Law Appendix (M.C. v
Bulgaria, O'Keeffe v Ireland).

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 3 - Sexual Assault and Aggravated Sexual
Assault
Sexual assault and aggravated sexual assault are non-penetrative sexual offences (or, where penetration
occurred but does not fall within the s.2 or s.4 rape framework). The principal statutes are s.2 and s.3 of the
Criminal Law (Rape)(Amendment) Act 1990. The consent framework under the 2017 Act applies.

3.1 Sexual Assault - s.2 CL(R)(A)A 1990
s.2 CL(R)(A)A 1990
(The 1990 Act renamed indecent assault as sexual assault and gender-neutralised the offence. The substantive
elements - assault accompanied by circumstances of indecency or sexuality - operate under the underlying
assault framework plus the sexual character of the act.)

Elements
Assault: Common-law / s.2 NFOAP framework - intentional or reckless force or threat thereof, or contact,
without consent.
Sexual character: The assault has a sexual character - sexual touching, exposure, contact of an inherently
sexual nature.
Without consent: The complainant did not consent within the s.48 CL(SO)A 2017 framework.
Mens rea: Knowledge or recklessness as to lack of consent (Murray-subjective).

Court and Penalty
Court: Indictable - Circuit Criminal Court; can be tried summarily.
Penalty: Up to 14 years on indictment (where complainant is 17 or over); up to 14 years where complainant is
under 17 (aggravated context); 12 months / fine summary.

Practical Scope
Sexual assault covers a wide range of conduct from non-consensual sexual touching (e.g., groping, sexual
contact short of penetration) to more serious sexual assaults. The wide range reflects the wide statutory
maximum and the importance of structured discretion at sentencing.

3.2 Aggravated Sexual Assault - s.3 CL(R)(A)A 1990
s.3 CL(R)(A)A 1990

Elements
Sexual assault: As under s.2.
Aggravating feature: (a) serious violence; OR (b) threat of serious violence; OR (c) injury, humiliation or
degradation of a grave nature.

Court and Penalty
Court: Central Criminal Court.
Penalty: Up to life imprisonment.

Operational Significance
Aggravated sexual assault is at the rape-equivalent end of the sentencing spectrum. Operationally, conduct that
involves serious violence, weapons, sustained degradation, or grave humiliation in a sexual context falls within
this provision. Where the conduct is non-penetrative but extremely serious, s.3 is the appropriate charge rather

than s.2.

3.3 Live Incident Response
As for rape (Chapter 2):
Private safe location for victim engagement.
Trauma-informed initial response.
BWV / contemporaneous notes.
Medical needs / SATU referral.
Forensic preservation - clothing, scene, no washing where possible.
Specialist Garda engagement (DARI / DSCB).
Victim support service referral.

3.4 Investigation Strategy
Same framework as rape investigation (Chapter 2.3):
Specialist victim interview.
SATU examination (same protocol; biological evidence may be present even in non-penetrative offences saliva, skin cells, hair).
Scene investigation.
Suspect investigation - arrest, clothing forensic, premises search, communications.
Third-party witnesses.
CCTV.

3.5 Suspect Strategy and Arrest Power
Specimen Arrest Words
'[Name], I am arresting you on suspicion of an offence of [sexual assault contrary to section 2 / aggravated
sexual assault contrary to section 3] of the Criminal Law (Rape)(Amendment) Act 1990. You are alleged to
have sexually assaulted [complainant] on [date] at [location] without her / his consent [for s.3 - in circumstances
involving serious violence / threat of serious violence / grave humiliation]. You are not obliged to say anything
unless you wish to do so but anything you do say will be taken down in writing and may be given in evidence.'

3.6 Interview Plan
As for rape - identification, relationship, account of relevant period, consent analysis, specific acts, force / threat
/ circumstances, communications, post-incident conduct.
For aggravated sexual assault - specifically engage the aggravating feature (serious violence / grave
humiliation).

3.7 PULSE / File Index
Same comprehensive file structure as rape (Chapter 2.6) adjusted for the offence type. Forensic component may
differ where the offence is non-penetrative (different biological evidence; injuries; clothing transfer; possible
saliva / DNA from intimate areas).

3.8 Charge Selection
Conduct

penetration

3.9 Defence Will Attack
Same vectors as rape (Chapter 2.9) - principally:
Consent - the principal defence.
Honest belief in consent.
Identification (in stranger cases).
Sexual character contested (e.g., the touching was incidental / accidental).
Aggravating feature contested (for s.3 - whether serious violence threshold met).
Fabrication.
Procedural challenge (JC).

3.10 Red Flag Escalation
As for rape - multiple complainants, weapons, vulnerable complainant, position of trust, DFSA, group offence,
historical disclosure.
Where conduct escalates from sexual assault to rape - charge both / principal charge accordingly.

3.11 Common Pitfalls
Under-charging - treating s.3 conduct as s.2 (the aggravating feature is operationally significant).
Failing to characterise sexual character clearly - the file must explain why the conduct was sexual.
Inadequate forensic - non-penetrative offences still generate forensic evidence.
As for rape - first response, SATU, specialist interview, communications preservation, multi-agency
engagement.

3.12 Ethics Touchpoints
Same as rape (Chapter 2.12).

3.13 Compliment Points / Cross-References
Strong sexual assault investigations - same scale as rape - formal commendation level.
Cross-reference: Ch.2 (rape framework); Ch.4 (consent); Ch.5 (child sexual offences); Ch.7 (investigation
framework); Volume 2 (NFOAP assault framework).

Chapter 4 - Consent under the 2017 Act
Section 48 of the Criminal Law (Sexual Offences) Act 2017 provides the modern Irish consent framework. The
provision is the operative test for consent in every sexual offence under the principal statutes (rape, sexual
assault, aggravated sexual assault, and others). This chapter treats the consent doctrine in operational depth the test, the indicators of non-consent, capacity, withdrawal, and the mistaken-belief defence.

4.1 The Operative Test - s.48(1)
s.48(1) CL(SO)A 2017
Two operative attributes: freely (voluntariness) and voluntarily (without compulsion). Both must be present.
The framing is positive - consent is something given, not something inferred from absence of refusal.

4.2 The Statutory Indicators of Non-Consent - s.48(2)
s.48(2) sets out circumstances in which a person does NOT consent:

(a) Force or Threat of Force
Force used against the person, or threat of force against the person or another. Threats need not be of immediate
force - threats of future violence, threats to family / others count.

(b) Unlawful Detention
Detention against the person's will. Operative even where the detention is not physically restraining - locked in
a room, taken to a location and prevented from leaving.

(c) Asleep or Unconscious
Including drug-induced unconsciousness, alcohol-induced unconsciousness, illness-related unconsciousness,
post-anaesthesia unconsciousness. A sleeping or unconscious person cannot consent. Resumption of
consciousness during the act does not constitute consent to what preceded.

(d) Incapable of Consenting Due to Intoxication
Intoxication that renders the person incapable of consenting. The threshold is incapacity - not 'any intoxication'.
A person too intoxicated to understand the situation or to communicate cannot consent. Capacity to consent is
the inquiry, not whether the person was sober. (See Capacity section below for detail.)

(e) Mistaken about Identity
The person is mistaken about the identity of the other party. Operationally relevant in spousal-deception cases
(impersonation by another person posing as a spouse) and certain other identity-deception scenarios.

(f) Mistaken about Nature of the Act
The person is mistaken about what the act is (e.g., believed it was medical examination; believed it was a
different kind of act).

(g) Submission as a Result of Words / Conduct Exploiting Dependence, Trust, or
Authority
Submission obtained through exploitation of a position of dependence, trust, or authority. Operative in:
Healthcare context - doctor, nurse, therapist exploiting patient.
Educational context - teacher exploiting student.
Religious context - cleric exploiting parishioner.
Workplace context - supervisor exploiting subordinate.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Care context - care worker exploiting service user.
Custodial context - prison officer exploiting prisoner.
Coercive control - DV context with sustained pattern of domination.

(h) Submission as a Result of Words or Conduct from the Other Person
Submission obtained by coercive words or conduct generally - even where not constituting explicit threat of
force. Operative in coercive sexual demands within relationships and other coercive contexts.

(i) Physically Incapable of Communicating Consent
Where the person is physically incapable of communicating consent - severe disability, severe intoxication
beyond unconsciousness threshold, certain medical conditions.

4.3 Capacity to Consent
Beyond the s.48(2) statutory indicators, capacity to consent is a separate analytical dimension. Three principal
capacity issues:

4.3.1 Intoxication Capacity
A person under the influence of alcohol / drugs may have impaired but not absent capacity, or may have
completely lost capacity. The investigation assesses:
Visible intoxication signs - speech, gait, coordination, awareness.
Volume / type of substance consumed.
Toxicology where DFSA suspected.
CCTV / witness evidence of presentation.
The point at which capacity was lost and whether sexual activity continued past that point.

4.3.2 Age Capacity
Persons under the age of consent (17 in Irish law for these purposes - though specific child sexual offences
apply under various age thresholds) cannot legally consent to sexual activity regardless of apparent agreement.
Chapter 5 treats the child sexual offence framework in detail. Operational point: investigation does not stop at
the under-age person's apparent agreement; the offence is committed regardless.

4.3.3 Mental Capacity
Persons with cognitive / intellectual disability or impairment may lack capacity. s.21 CL(SO)A 2017
specifically addresses sexual offences against vulnerable persons:
Defines 'protected person' as a person with mental or intellectual disability or mental illness of such nature or
degree as to render them incapable of (a) understanding the nature, or the reasonably foreseeable consequences,
of a sexual act, or (b) evaluating relevant information for the purposes of deciding whether or not to engage in
such an act, or (c) communicating their consent.
Sexual contact with a protected person is an offence regardless of apparent consent.

4.4 Withdrawal of Consent
Consent can be withdrawn at any point. The 2017 Act framework recognises that consent is ongoing - it relates
to the act at the time. Operative implications:
Consent to one act is not consent to another.
Consent to penetration is not consent to a different sexual act.
Consent at one moment is not consent at a later moment.
Withdrawal can be expressed verbally, physically (pulling away), or through cessation of participation.
Continuation of sexual activity after withdrawal of consent is non-consensual - the elements of the offence are
made out for the post-withdrawal acts.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

4.5 The Mistaken-Belief Defence
The mens rea of rape includes knowledge or recklessness as to non-consent. Where the accused contends he
honestly believed the complainant was consenting, the defence engages. The doctrine:

4.5.1 The Pre-2017 Position - Honest Belief
Historically, the mistaken-belief defence required the belief to be honestly held - subjective. The Irish position
drew on the English Morgan doctrine. The 2017 Act preserves the honest-belief framework but introduces
consideration of reasonableness.

4.5.2 The 2017 Act Position
s.48(8) CL(SO)A 2017
Two limbs:
Honest belief - the accused actually believed there was consent (subjective).
Reasonable in the circumstances - the belief was reasonable (objective).

4.5.3 The s.48(9) Factors
s.48(9) directs that in determining whether the belief was reasonable, regard shall be had to all the circumstances
including any steps the accused took to ascertain whether the other person was consenting.
Operational implication: the prosecution case is supported by evidence of:
Indicators of non-consent that were objectively apparent (intoxication, force, threat, etc.).
What steps (if any) the accused took to confirm consent.
The reasonableness of inferring consent in the circumstances.

4.6 Operational Application - Building the Consent Element
In rape and sexual assault investigations, the consent element is often the principal contested issue. The
investigation builds the case for non-consent through:

4.6.1 The Complainant's Account
Specific articulation of non-consent - words used, conduct demonstrating non-consent.
State of capacity at relevant time.
Circumstances of submission (s.48(2) indicators).
Withdrawal of consent (where applicable).

4.6.2 Corroborating Evidence of Non-Consent
Injuries consistent with non-consent (defensive injuries, restraint marks).
Forensic - signs of force, signs of struggle.
CCTV / witness - the complainant's presentation before and after.
Communications - the complainant's communications with others before, during, after.
Recent complaint - to whom the complainant disclosed.
Toxicology - intoxication impacting capacity.
Suspect's account / post-incident conduct - flight, communications, attempts to influence.

4.6.3 Building Against the Mistaken-Belief Defence
Indicators that should have made the accused aware (objectively apparent intoxication, absence of response,
expressions of unwillingness).
Steps the accused did not take (no inquiry, no checking, no pause).
The accused's awareness of vulnerability.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Communications evidence of accused's mindset.

4.7 Specific Scenarios
4.7.1 Drug-Facilitated Sexual Assault (DFSA)
Cases where the complainant was incapacitated by drugs (administered or self-administered). Operational
protocol:
Urgent toxicology - SATU urinalysis early; blood / hair sampling for retrospective analysis.
Substance identification - GHB, ketamine, prescription sedatives, alcohol-drug combinations.
Source of substance - was it administered by the suspect (which is itself an offence) or consumed by the
complainant in unsuspecting circumstances.
CCTV showing intoxication progression.
Witness accounts of complainant's presentation.
Suspect's awareness of complainant's state.

4.7.2 Spousal / Partner Rape
Marital rape exemption was abolished in 1990. Rape within marriage / civil partnership / intimate relationship
is rape. Operational considerations:
DV-context investigation framework (Volume 4) operates.
Coercive control context may be present (s.39 DV Act 2018).
Charge stacking - rape + s.39 + applicable assaults / threats.
Witness intimidation considerations - extended-family, financial, social pressure.

4.7.3 Acquaintance Rape
Rape by a person known to the complainant (the most common scenario). The consent element is typically the
contested issue (identification is rarely in doubt). Investigation focuses on:
Account of the meeting / relationship.
Communications before, during, after.
Witness observations.
Circumstances of the alleged sexual activity.
Complainant's account of non-consent.
Suspect's account.

4.7.4 Stranger Rape
Rape by an unknown person. Less common but heightened operational urgency:
Identification investigation - DNA, CCTV, witness descriptions, VIPER.
Geographic profiling where pattern offending suspected.
Specialist DSCB / DOCB engagement.
Public safety considerations - the suspect remains at large.

4.7.5 Group / Multiple-Suspect Sexual Offences
Each suspect individually responsible for the act they committed. Joint enterprise considerations operate where
one suspect supports / encourages another's offence. Each suspect investigated individually:
Specific conduct of each.
Specific communications.
Specific forensic links.
Specific custody / interview process.

Chapter 5 - Sexual Offences Against Children
Sexual offences against children engage a distinct statutory framework with age-specific offences and
aggravated penalties. The principal statutes are the Criminal Law (Sexual Offences) Act 2006 (which created
the defilement offences under-17 / under-15) and the Criminal Law (Sexual Offences) Act 2017 (which
extended and modernised the framework, adding online grooming and other provisions). This chapter sets out
the framework with operational application.
Operational Priority
Sexual offences against children engage immediate and sustained operational priority. Tusla / Children First
mandatory reporting; protection of the child; preservation of evidence; specialist interview by trained Garda;
multi-agency coordination. The criminal investigation operates alongside the child welfare framework - not in
tension with it.

5.1 Age of Consent and the Framework
The age of consent for sexual activity in Irish law is 17. Sexual activity with a person under 17 is an offence
regardless of apparent consent. The framework has additional protective layers for younger children (under 15)
and aggravated provisions for very young children.

5.1.1 The Age Thresholds
Age

Framework

Under 13

Aggravated provisions; absolute liability features

Under 15

s.16 CL(SO)A 2006 framework - 'defilement of child under 15'

Under 17

s.17 CL(SO)A 2006 / s.18 CL(SO)A 2017 framework - 'defilement of child under 17'

17 and over

Standard adult sexual offences framework (Ch.2-4)

5.2 Defilement of Child Under 17 - s.17 CL(SO)A 2006 / Reformed
Framework
s.17 CL(SO)A 2006 (as updated)

Elements
Sexual act: Defined broadly - includes any act of sexual penetration; can also extend to non-penetrative sexual
acts under the 2017 Act framework.
Child under 17: At the time of the act.
Apparent consent immaterial: The offence is committed regardless of the child's apparent agreement.
Mistake-of-age defence: Limited - the accused may have a defence if he honestly believed the child was 17 or
over AND took reasonable steps to ascertain age. The defence is fact-sensitive and not always available.

Penalty
Court: Indictable.
Penalty: Up to 5 years on indictment (general); up to 7 years where the accused is in a position of trust /
authority over the child.

Position of Trust Aggravation
Where the accused is in a position of trust or authority (teacher, religious figure, coach, healthcare worker,
family member, etc.) over the child, aggravated penalties apply. Operationally significant for cases involving

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

educational, religious, sporting, or care settings.

5.3 Defilement of Child Under 15 - s.16 CL(SO)A 2006
s.16 CL(SO)A 2006

Penalty
Court: Indictable.
Penalty: Up to life imprisonment.

Operational Significance
The framework recognises the increased gravity of sexual acts with younger children by providing the
maximum penalty (life). The under-15 threshold marks a substantial escalation in sentencing exposure
compared to the under-17 offence.

5.4 Aggravated Provisions - Children Under 13
Sexual offences against very young children (under 13) engage the most serious sentencing exposure. Specific
aggravated provisions and substantial sentencing apply. The investigation engages the full specialist apparatus
- DSCB, specialist interviewer, Tusla, multi-agency response.

5.5 Sexual Exploitation of a Child - CL(SO)A 2017
S.10 CL(SO)A 2017 (and surrounding provisions) creates offences of sexual exploitation of children. The
framework covers:
Causing or inciting a child to engage in sexual activity.
Causing a child to watch sexual activity.
Causing a child to engage in pornographic performance.
Sexual exploitation in the context of prostitution / trafficking (overlap with the Criminal Law (Human
Trafficking) Act 2008).

5.6 Online Grooming - s.8 CL(SO)A 2017
s.8 CL(SO)A 2017

Elements
Communication: Via ICT - any electronic communication (text, messaging app, social media, gaming
platform, email).
With a child: Person under 17.
For the purpose of facilitating sexual exploitation: The accused's purpose - established by the content of
communications, the pattern of approach, the steps taken.

Operational Application
Sting / undercover operations - Garda investigators may pose as children online (within specific operational
frameworks). Specialist DSCB engagement.
Reported grooming - parent/guardian or victim reports grooming behaviour.
Disclosure during other investigation - grooming discovered during search of devices, etc.
Multi-jurisdictional - grooming often crosses national boundaries; international cooperation (Europol,
INHOPE).
Forensic preservation - device seizure, comprehensive forensic examination.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Penalty
Up to 14 years on indictment under s.8 CL(SO)A 2017.

5.7 Causing a Child to Watch Sexual Activity
Specific offences under the 2017 Act covering causing a child to watch sexual activity / pornographic material.
The conduct, separate from physical sexual contact, is itself recognised as a sexual offence against the child.

5.8 Investigation Framework - Child Sexual Offences
5.8.1 First Disclosure
Children's disclosures of sexual abuse often come through:
Direct disclosure to a parent / family member, who reports to Gardai.
Disclosure to a teacher, who reports under Children First.
Disclosure to a healthcare professional, who reports under Children First.
Disclosure during examination by social worker (Tusla).
Disclosure at Garda Station accompanied by parent.
Discovery during investigation of unrelated matter (e.g., adult disclosing during a different report).

5.8.2 The Initial Response
Listen, do not lead. The child's own account is critical and must not be tainted by leading questions.
Note verbatim - the disclosure as it was made; in the child's own words; with date, time, audience.
Child welfare first - is the child safe, in immediate need of medical care, in continuing risk.
Tusla / Children First report - mandatory; immediate.
Do not interview the child substantively at first contact - wait for specialist interviewer.
Engage specialist DSCB / Children's Interview Suite.

5.8.3 Specialist Interview
Trained specialist Garda interviewer.
Garda Children's Interview Suite (or Tusla equivalent facility).
Video-recorded interview under CEA 1992 framework - admissible as evidence-in-chief at trial.
Age-appropriate questioning - structured approaches (NICHD, etc.) used by specialist interviewers.
Appropriate adult / family / Tusla worker present where appropriate.
Multiple sessions may be needed - planned and structured.

5.8.4 Forensic / Medical Examination
SATU or specialist pediatric forensic examination.
Child SATU referral procedures.
Sensitive examination procedures appropriate to child.
Findings interpreted in pediatric context (different from adult).
Medical care continues alongside evidential collection.

5.8.5 Multi-Agency
Tusla - lead child welfare; care arrangements, family work.
CARI - specialist child sexual abuse support service.
Healthcare - pediatric mental health, ongoing medical.
School - support, attendance, awareness within confidentiality.
Joint protocols - Garda / Tusla joint working arrangements at District level.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

5.8.6 Suspect Investigation
Same as adult sexual offence framework (Ch.2.3-4) with these additional dimensions:
Position of trust analysis - was the suspect in such a position; if so, aggravation.
Device forensic - typically extensive in modern child sexual offences (online aspect, communications, images).
Workplace / institutional investigation where relevant - school, club, religious organisation.
Other potential victims - same suspect may have offended against multiple children.

5.9 Historical Child Sexual Abuse (CSA) Cases
Adult disclosures of childhood sexual abuse are a substantial portion of sexual offences investigation. The
framework:

5.9.1 Operational Reality
Disclosure may occur decades after the offence.
Memory of the complainant - subject to time but Irish courts have been receptive to historical complaints.
Corroborating evidence often limited - witnesses elderly or deceased; physical evidence not preserved;
documentary record limited.
Other complainants may exist - same suspect across multiple victims.
Institutional context common - schools, religious institutions, care homes.

5.9.2 Investigative Approach
Same first-response and specialist interview framework as current cases.
Detailed antecedent investigation - was the suspect in a position with access to children; what was the context.
Other-complainant inquiries - sensitive, careful, structured.
Institutional records where they survive.
Witness inquiries - family members, contemporaries, professionals who had relevant contact.
Care with leading - the danger of contamination across complainants must be managed by separate, structured
investigation.

5.9.3 Legal Issues
Delay - not a bar to prosecution in itself; but may engage fairness considerations addressed at trial.
Burden remains on prosecution to prove beyond reasonable doubt.
Multiple complainants may give rise to similar fact / pattern evidence considerations (Volume 2 Evidence
Appendix).
Statutory limitation periods do not apply to most sexual offences.

5.10 Position of Trust Offences
Where the accused is in a position of trust over the child victim, aggravated offences and penalties engage:
Teacher / educator.
Religious figure / clergy.
Sports coach.
Healthcare professional.
Care worker.
Foster parent / step-parent.
Other family member (with specific incest framework under CL(SO)(A)A 2007).
The investigation engages the institutional / contextual dimension - what was the access, what was the
supervision, what other potential victims may exist.

5.11 Garda Sex Offender Management Units

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Convicted sex offenders are subject to the Sex Offenders Act 2001 notification regime. Garda Sex Offender
Management Units manage notifications, address verification, travel notification, risk assessment.
Operationally engaged in:
Post-conviction registration.
Ongoing monitoring.
Civil orders (Sex Offenders Order).
Disclosure to relevant institutions where statutorily authorised.
(Sex Offenders Act framework in detail in Ch.7 of this volume.)

Chapter 6 - Specialist Sexual Offences
This chapter covers specialist sexual offences that operate alongside the principal rape / sexual assault / child
sexual offence framework: child sexual abuse material (CSAM); sex trafficking; image-based sexual abuse;
sexual exploitation in prostitution-context; female genital mutilation; sexual offences in custodial / institutional
contexts.

6.1 Child Sexual Abuse Material (CSAM)
The Child Trafficking and Pornography Act 1998 (as amended) is the principal Irish statute on what is now
termed Child Sexual Abuse Material (replacing the historical term 'child pornography' which understates the
harm). The 2017 Act adds further provisions.

6.1.1 The Principal Offences
Production / making of CSAM.
Distribution / supply / advertisement of CSAM.
Possession of CSAM.
Causing or inciting a child to be involved in production of CSAM.
Knowingly accessing CSAM online (added by later amendments).

6.1.2 Investigation Framework
Specialist Garda Cyber Crime Bureau (GCCB) and DSCB engagement.
Device forensic - phones, computers, storage media, cloud accounts.
International cooperation - Interpol Image Database, INHOPE, Europol.
Hash-value analysis - known CSAM hashes for identification.
Victim identification work - international protocols for identifying children in material.
Garda welfare support - exposure to material is occupationally traumatic.

6.1.3 Possession Cases - Common Path
Internet Service Provider report - flagged content.
Mutual legal assistance - referral from foreign authority.
Discovery during other investigation.
Workplace / device discovery and reporting.
Search warrant on premises (Damache); device seizure; forensic examination.
Hash matching against known material; identification of any unique material.
Suspect interview - knowledge, source, distribution.
Charge selection - possession (most common); distribution if evidence supports; production / contact with
children if applicable.

6.1.4 Penalty
Possession - up to 5 years on indictment.
Distribution / production - substantially higher; up to life for some categories.

6.2 Sex Trafficking
The Criminal Law (Human Trafficking) Act 2008 and the Criminal Law (Human Trafficking) (Amendment)
Act 2013 are the principal trafficking statutes. They cover trafficking for sexual exploitation and other purposes.

6.2.1 The Trafficking Offence
Trafficking for sexual exploitation involves: (a) recruitment, transportation, transfer, harbouring, or reception

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

of a person; (b) by means of threat, force, coercion, abduction, fraud, deception, abuse of power, abuse of
vulnerability, or payment; (c) for the purpose of sexual exploitation. The threshold elements operate together.

6.2.2 Operational Application
Specialist Garda HR and Garda National Bureau (DSCB).
International cooperation - source country, transit, destination.
Victim identification - victims may not present as such; trafficked persons typically have profound trust issues.
Multi-agency engagement - victim services, immigration, welfare.
Charge stacking - trafficking + applicable sexual offences against victims + organised crime + money
laundering.

6.2.3 Victim Status
Trafficking victims have specific protective status under the framework.
Recovery and reflection period - statutory provision for victims to be supported without immediate immigration
/ prosecution pressure.
Non-punishment principle - victims not prosecuted for offences committed as a direct consequence of being
trafficked.

6.3 Image-Based Sexual Abuse (Coco's Law) - HHCROA 2020
The Harassment, Harmful Communications and Related Offences Act 2020 ('Coco's Law' - named for Nicole
'Coco' Fox) criminalises non-consensual distribution of intimate images.

6.3.1 The Offences
s.2 - Threatening / Grossly Offensive Communications
Threatening / grossly offensive communications by any means with intent to cause harm or being reckless as
to such harm.
s.3 - Distribution / Publication / Threat with Intent to Harm
Distribution, publication, or threat to distribute / publish intimate images of another person, without consent,
with intent to cause harm or being reckless as to such harm. Penalty - up to 7 years on indictment.
s.4 - Distribution / Publication without Consent (Lower Threshold)
Distribution / publication of intimate image without consent of the person concerned. Lower threshold than s.3
- does not require intent to harm. Penalty - up to 12 months / fine summary.

6.3.2 'Intimate Image' Definition
Image / video depicting genitalia, breasts (if female), buttocks, or sexual act. Includes images created from real
images (digitally altered to depict the person).

6.3.3 Operational Application
Common in post-DV separation contexts (cross-reference Volume 4).
Common in extortion / 'sextortion' contexts (cross-reference s.17 CJ(PO)A 1994).
Preservation of digital evidence essential and urgent (deletion risk).
Multi-platform investigation - the image may be on multiple platforms / shared widely.
Identification of distribution sources / chains.
Victim support engagement - the trauma is significant; specialist support.

6.4 Sexual Exploitation in Prostitution Context
The Criminal Law (Sexual Offences) Act 2017 introduced the 'Nordic-style' approach - criminalising the
purchase of sexual services while decriminalising the sale (in most circumstances). Key provisions:

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Purchase of sexual services - offence by the purchaser.
Brothel-keeping / organising prostitution - offence (more serious where children involved).
Trafficking for sexual exploitation - operates alongside (CL(HT)A 2008).
Persons selling sexual services - generally not criminalised under the 2017 Act framework.

6.5 Female Genital Mutilation - CJ(FGM)A 2012
The Criminal Justice (Female Genital Mutilation) Act 2012 criminalises FGM and related conduct:
Performing or attempting to perform FGM in the State.
Removing a child from the State for the purpose of FGM.
Aiding / abetting / counselling / procuring FGM.
Multi-agency response - Tusla, healthcare, community engagement. Often a hidden offence; community-based
investigation. Charge selection - FGM offence + child cruelty / assault as appropriate.

6.6 Sexual Offences in Custodial / Institutional Contexts
Sexual contact between custodial staff and detained persons - specific aggravated offences.
Sexual offences in healthcare settings.
Sexual offences in care contexts (vulnerable adults / children).
Position-of-trust aggravation operates throughout. Institutional disclosure protocols engage. Specialist
investigative engagement.

Chapter 7 - Investigation Framework
This chapter consolidates the operational practice across sexual offences investigation: victim engagement;
SATU; specialist Garda units; forensic strategy; historical / disclosure CSA cases; suspect investigation; Sex
Offender Register management.

7.1 Garda Specialist Resources
7.1.1 Divisional Level
Domestic Abuse / Sexual Crime Investigation Units (DARI) - District / Divisional-level specialist resource for
sexual crime.
Specialist Victim Interviewers - trained Gardai for victim interview in sexual offences.
Garda Specialist Interview Suites - facilities for victim interview.

7.1.2 National Level
Garda National Protective Services Bureau (DSCB) - national specialist unit for serious sexual crime, child
protection, online child exploitation.
Garda National Cyber Crime Bureau (GCCB) - digital forensic, CSAM, online grooming, image-based abuse.
Garda Sex Offender Management Units - post-conviction monitoring.

7.2 SATU - Sexual Assault Treatment Units
Six SATUs operate across the State (Dublin Rotunda, Cork SAATU, Galway, Letterkenny, Mullingar,
Waterford). The SATU framework:
Clinical examination by trained SATU clinician.
Sexual Offences Examination Kit (SOEK) - standardised evidence collection.
Photography of injuries with clinical accuracy.
Medical care - STI screening, post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP), emergency contraception, mental health
support.
Continued care - SATU follow-up appointments.
Documentation - SATU forensic report becomes evidence.
Clinician as witness - SATU clinicians give evidence at trial.
Sample storage option - victims who have not decided to report can have evidence collected and stored pending
decision (with statutory time limit on storage).

7.3 Forensic Strategy
7.3.1 Biological Evidence
DNA - body sites (vaginal, anal, oral, body surface) and clothing / scene.
Saliva - bite marks, kissing, oral sex.
Semen - vaginal, anal, oral, clothing, scene.
Blood - from injuries, from suspect (defensive wounds), scene.
Hair - transferred during contact.
Skin cells - direct skin contact.

7.3.2 Time Windows
Vaginal samples - typically up to 72 hours, longer in some cases.
Anal samples - typically up to 72 hours.
Oral samples - shorter window (up to 24-48 hours, less reliable).

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Skin / clothing samples - longer windows possible.
Hair samples - extended window.
SATU clinician advises on appropriate sampling based on time elapsed and nature of contact.

7.3.3 Trace Evidence
Fibres - clothing transfer.
Glass / paint / debris - scene-specific.
Lubricant residues - in penetrative offences.

7.3.4 Suspect Forensic
Clothing seizure.
Body samples (intimate samples require consent / authorisation; CJA 2007 framework).
Injury photography (defensive wounds from victim resistance).
Premises / vehicle search for evidence.

7.4 Historical / Disclosure CSA - Investigation Protocol
Historical Child Sexual Abuse cases are a substantial portion of sexual offences investigation. The framework
adapts the standard sexual offences approach to the time-delay context:
Initial disclosure handled by trained specialist - listening, non-leading.
Multi-session statement-taking - the account develops.
Specific interview structure for disclosing adult victims of CSA.
Antecedent investigation of suspect - context, access, opportunity.
Other-complainant investigation - structured, separate, careful to avoid contamination.
Institutional records (school, religious, residential institution) where surviving.
Multi-agency engagement - mental health support for complainants.
Specialist support service liaison - One in Four, Dublin Rape Crisis Centre, CARI for survivors.

7.5 Suspect Investigation
Standard arrest, custody, interview framework with sexual offences specifics.
Pre-interview consultation (Gormley) - typically requested and facilitated.
Solicitor presence in interview (Doyle) - typically requested and facilitated.
Suspect SATU-style examination - for biological evidence; with consent or court-ordered.
Premises / device search (Damache compliance).
Communications evidence.
Inference framework (s.18 / 19 / 19A CJA 1984) - Buck-strict compliance.
Custody welfare - even where the offence is serious, the suspect's welfare in custody is the standard.

7.6 The Sex Offender Register - Sex Offenders Act 2001
Persons convicted of specified sexual offences are subject to notification requirements under the Sex Offenders
Act 2001 (as amended).

7.6.1 The Notification Regime
Registered offender must notify Gardai of: name; date of birth; address; any change of address; intended travel
out of jurisdiction.
Initial notification within statutory period of release / conviction.
Ongoing notification of changes.
Duration of notification - varies by sentence (lifetime for life sentence; specified periods for lesser sentences).

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

7.6.2 Garda Management
Sex Offender Management Units track and manage registered offenders.
Address verification - periodic visits to confirm details.
Risk assessment and intervention.
Investigation of breaches - failure to notify is an offence.

7.6.3 Sex Offenders Order
Sex Offenders Order is a civil order available where a registered offender's conduct gives cause for concern prohibits specified conduct (e.g., contact with children, frequenting specified places). Breach is an offence.

7.6.4 Disclosure Considerations
Information about registered offenders is confidential.
Limited disclosure to bodies with statutory responsibility for children / vulnerable adults may operate within
frameworks.
Public disclosure not generally provided - the framework is for risk management, not public notification.

Appendix - Defences, Burdens, Charge Selection, File
Craft (Sexual Offences)
Burdens framework carries from Volume 2. Sexual offences specific considerations on charge selection, file
craft, and the defences likely to be raised.

Part A - Burdens of Proof
Standard criminal burdens. Prosecution proves every element beyond reasonable doubt. Mistaken-belief
defence under s.48(8) CL(SO)A 2017 - evidential burden on accused to put the defence in play; legal burden
on prosecution to disprove the honest and reasonable belief.

Part B - The Principal Defences
B.1 Consent
The principal defence in adult sexual offence cases. The accused contends the complainant consented. Rebut
by: complainant's account; s.48(2) statutory indicators (force / threat / detention / sleep / intoxication / mistake
/ dependence / submission / inability to communicate); corroborating evidence (forensic, witness,
communications, contemporaneous behaviour); recent complaint; absence of identifiable motive to fabricate.

B.2 Honest and Reasonable Mistake of Consent - s.48(8) CL(SO)A 2017
The accused contends he honestly and reasonably believed there was consent. Rebut by: objectively apparent
indicators of non-consent; the accused's awareness of intoxication / vulnerability / dependence / coercion;
absence of any steps to ascertain consent; the unreasonableness of the asserted belief in the circumstances.

B.3 Identification
In stranger cases. Rebut by: DNA, forensic links, CCTV, identification procedures (VIPER), description
quality, ADVOKATE assessment.

B.4 Fabrication / Motive to Lie
Defence suggestion of complaint as fabrication. Rebut by: corroborating evidence (forensic, witness,
communications); contemporaneous recent complaint; absence of identifiable motive; the complainant's own
credibility (subject to trauma-informed analysis of inconsistencies).

B.5 Procedural Challenges (JC Path)
Routine in sexual offences cases - SATU evidence handling, chain of custody, custody compliance, interview
procedural compliance (Gormley, Doyle, Buck), warrant compliance (Damache), search and seizure
framework. Rebut by procedural rigour at every step.

B.6 Inconsistency
Defence probing inconsistencies in complainant's account across statements / interviews / testimony. Rebut by:
trauma-informed expert evidence where appropriate; the centrality vs periphery distinction (inconsistency in
peripheral detail does not undermine credibility on central facts); the multi-session statement-taking explains
evolution of account.

B.7 Delayed Disclosure (Historical Cases)
Defence suggestion that delay undermines credibility. Rebut by: recognised reasons for delayed disclosure in
sexual offences cases (shame, fear, dependence on suspect, denial, dissociation); absence of motive to fabricate
at the time of disclosure; corroborating evidence; institutional / contextual evidence.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

B.8 Specific to Child Sexual Offences
Mistake of age (where statutorily available) - rebut by: complainant's actual age provable; suspect's awareness;
absence of steps to ascertain age.
No sexual character contended (where conduct alleged to be non-sexual) - rebut by: context, communications,
suspect statements.

Part D - Charge Stacking in Sexual Offences
Sexual offences frequently involve multiple charges:
Rape + assault / false imprisonment / threats / criminal damage (where conduct exceeds the sexual offence
itself).
Rape + s.39 DV Act 2018 coercive control (where intimate partner / DV context).
Multiple rape / sexual assault counts (where multiple acts or occasions).
Sexual assault + position of trust aggravation (where applicable).
Child sexual offences + grooming / online offences (where the path involved both).
CSAM + contact sexual offences (where the suspect produced material from offences he committed).
Sex trafficking + applicable sexual offences against trafficked victims.

Appendix - Evidence and Admissibility (Sexual Offences)
Part A - The s.3 CL(R)A 1981 Framework on Sexual History Evidence
Section 3 of the Criminal Law (Rape) Act 1981 (as amended) restricts cross-examination of a complainant on
previous sexual experience without leave of the court. The framework:
General prohibition on cross-examination of complainant on sexual history with persons other than the accused.
Application required for leave; complainant's interests considered.
Leave granted only where exclusion would render trial unfair.
Limited application to history with the accused himself - the prior relationship may be relevant in some cases.
Operational implication: complainant's sexual history is not generally an evidential battleground.

Part B - Recent Complaint
Doctrine substantially modernised in Irish practice. The complainant's prior complaint to a third party may be
admissible:
As part of the narrative.
To rebut suggestions of recent fabrication.
Where contemporaneity supports credibility.
Modern practice recognises that 'recent' is interpreted flexibly - the complaint need not be immediate;
significant delays do not preclude admissibility. Take statements from disclosure recipients early.

Part C - Anonymity Protections
S.7 CL(R)A 1981 (as amended):

Part A - Rape and Sexual Assault
A.1 s.2 Criminal Law (Rape) Act 1981 - Rape
s.2(1): A man commits rape if (a) he has unlawful sexual intercourse with a woman who at the time of the
intercourse does not consent to it, and (b) at that time he knows that she does not consent to the intercourse or
he is reckless as to whether she does or does not consent to it.
Penalty: Up to life imprisonment.

A.2 s.4 Criminal Law (Rape)(Amendment) Act 1990 - Section 4 Rape
Sexual assault that includes (a) penetration (however slight) of the anus or mouth by the penis, or (b) penetration
(however slight) of the vagina by any object held or manipulated by another person.
Penalty: Up to life imprisonment.

A.3 s.2 Criminal Law (Rape)(Amendment) Act 1990 - Sexual Assault
s.2(1): The offence of indecent assault upon any male person and the offence of indecent assault upon any
female person shall be known as sexual assault.
Penalty: Up to 14 years on indictment (where complainant is 17+); up to 14 yrs (where under 17, position of
trust aggravation may apply).

A.4 s.3 Criminal Law (Rape)(Amendment) Act 1990 - Aggravated Sexual Assault
s.3: A sexual assault that involves serious violence or the threat of serious violence or is such as to cause injury,
humiliation or degradation of a grave nature to the person assaulted.
Penalty: Up to life imprisonment.

Part B - Consent (s.48 CL(SO)A 2017)
s.48(1): A person consents to a sexual act if he or she freely and voluntarily agrees to engage in that act.
s.48(2): A person does not consent to a sexual act if (a) he or she permits the act to take place or submits to it
because of force, threat of force, or detention; (b) he or she is asleep or unconscious; (c) he or she is incapable
of consenting because of the effect of alcohol or other drug; (d) he or she is suffering from a physical disability
which prevents him or her from communicating consent; (e) he or she is mistaken as to the nature and quality
of the act or the identity of any person involved; (f) he or she is being unlawfully detained; (g) he or she submits
because of words or conduct that exploit a position of dependence, trust or authority; (h) other circumstances
as set out in the section.
s.48(8): A man does not commit an offence of rape under section 2 of the 1981 Act if at the time of the alleged
offence the accused honestly believed that, at the time of the alleged offence, the woman was consenting and
the belief was reasonable in the circumstances.
s.48(9): In determining whether the belief was reasonable, regard shall be had to all the circumstances,
including any steps the accused took to ascertain whether the other person was consenting.

s.16: A person who engages in a sexual act with a child who is under the age of 15 years shall be guilty of an

Appendix - Case Law Resource (Vol 6 - Sexual Offences)
Sexual offences case law spans the Irish procedural authorities that carry from Volume 2, the consent
jurisprudence developing under the 2017 Act, and the distinctive ECtHR positive-obligations line on State
investigation of sexual offences (M.C. v Bulgaria; O'Keeffe v Ireland). This appendix treats the principal
authorities in vLex-style depth.
Source verification reminder
Sexual offences case law continues to develop. The 2017 Act consent jurisprudence is still maturing; historical
case law on the pre-2017 consent doctrine remains relevant for transitional / historical cases. Verify all citations
on vLex Ireland, BAILII, HUDOC (ECtHR) before relying.

Part A - ECtHR Authority on State Positive Obligations
A.1 X and Y v The Netherlands (1985) 8 EHRR 235 - Foundational
Citation: Application No. 8978/80, judgment 26 March 1985 (ECtHR).
Facts: Y was a young woman with intellectual disability who was sexually assaulted in the care institution
where she lived. Dutch criminal law required the victim personally to file a complaint for prosecution; Y was
deemed incapable of doing so and her father's complaint was held insufficient. The case challenged the
procedural gap on Convention grounds.
Holding: Violation of Article 8 (right to private life). The State's positive obligations under Article 8 include
providing effective criminal-law protection against sexual abuse, particularly for persons unable to protect
themselves.
Reasoning: The Court found that the gap in Dutch criminal procedure - preventing prosecution where the
victim could not personally complain - failed the State's positive obligation. Article 8 requires effective
deterrence and prosecution of sexual offences.
Significance: Foundational ECtHR authority on State positive obligations in sexual offences. Recognises that
adequate criminal law protection is part of Article 8.
Operational Application: The framework establishes that the State (and Garda Siochana as part of it) must
provide effective criminal-law protection. Operational deficiency in the investigation of sexual offences against
vulnerable persons engages Convention review. The framework supports the operational priority given to
sexual offences against vulnerable victims.

A.2 M.C. v Bulgaria (2003) Application No. 39272/98
Citation: Application No. 39272/98, judgment 4 December 2003 (ECtHR).
Facts: M.C. was a 14-year-old girl in Bulgaria who alleged she had been raped by two men. Bulgarian criminal
law and prosecution practice required proof of physical resistance for rape - the prosecution found insufficient
evidence of resistance and the case did not proceed. M.C. brought proceedings against Bulgaria alleging the
State had failed to provide effective protection against sexual violence.
Holding: Violations of Article 3 (prohibition of inhuman / degrading treatment) and Article 8 (private life).
Bulgaria failed its positive obligations to effectively investigate and prosecute sexual offences. The requirement
of physical resistance was incompatible with Convention obligations - modern understanding of sexual violence
recognises that victims often do not resist physically (freezing, fear, tonic immobility) but this does not
constitute consent.
Reasoning: The Court emphasised the State's positive obligations include not only criminalising sexual
offences but ensuring the criminal law and prosecution practice respond to the reality of sexual violence. The
'resistance' approach was outmoded; consent-based approaches were the modern Convention-compliant
standard. The investigation and prosecution must be effective in fact, not just in formal availability.
Significance: Landmark ECtHR authority on the operational requirements of sexual offences investigation.
Establishes that:
Consent (not resistance) is the operative concept.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

State investigation must engage realistically with the dynamics of sexual violence (freezing, submission,
intoxication).
Effective investigation is a Convention obligation.
The State response to non-physical-resistance cases must be substantively engaged.
Operational Application: The M.C. framework operates throughout sexual offences investigation. (a)
Investigation must engage with cases where the complainant did not resist physically - the absence of resistance
does not negate non-consent; (b) Specialist Garda training and resourcing are part of the State's compliance; (c)
The s.48 CL(SO)A 2017 consent framework is aligned with M.C. and supports effective investigation; (d)
Trauma-informed practice (recognising freeze response, fragmented memory, delayed disclosure) is a
Convention-supported standard, not optional.

A.3 O'Keeffe v Ireland [GC] (2014) Application No. 35810/09 - LANDMARK FOR
IRELAND
Citation: Application No. 35810/09, Grand Chamber judgment 28 January 2014 (ECtHR).
Facts: Louise O'Keeffe was sexually abused as a child in the 1970s by the principal of her national school. The
State conceded the abuse occurred. Her civil action against the State for failure to protect her was unsuccessful
in the Irish courts. She brought proceedings against Ireland alleging the State had failed in its positive
obligations under Articles 3 and 13 ECHR.
Holding: Violation of Article 3 (substantive limb - failure to protect from inhuman / degrading treatment) and
Article 13 (lack of effective remedy). Ireland was found to have failed in its positive obligation to protect
children from sexual abuse in primary schools, given the State's role in providing primary education through
the (then) management arrangements.
Reasoning: The Grand Chamber emphasised that Article 3 imposes positive obligations on States to take
measures to protect persons from inhuman / degrading treatment, including by third parties. In the school
context with State-funded education, the State had specific protective obligations. The Court found that
mechanisms for detection / response to abuse in primary schools were insufficient at the relevant period. The
Article 13 remedy gap reflected the absence of a viable domestic action against the State.
Significance: Landmark for Ireland. Confirms State positive obligations in respect of child sexual abuse in
institutional contexts. Has driven substantial reform of child protection frameworks in Ireland. The Children
First Act 2015 and the modern child protection apparatus operate against the background of O'Keeffe.
Operational Application: (a) Children First mandatory reporting framework is part of Ireland's Convention
compliance; (b) Garda investigation of historical child sexual abuse engages the State's positive obligation
framework; (c) Multi-agency child protection coordination (Garda, Tusla, schools, healthcare) operates within
the post-O'Keeffe framework; (d) Where systemic gaps in child protection are identified, raising the issue
through appropriate channels supports the State's compliance posture.

A.4 Aydin v Turkey (1997) 25 EHRR 251
Citation: Application No. 23178/94, judgment 25 September 1997.
Significance: Article 3 - rape in custodial / State context is torture under Article 3. Investigation obligation
engaged. Operational application: sexual offences committed in custodial / state-actor contexts engage
particularly intense Convention review; the investigation must be independent, effective, and prompt.

A.5 I.G. v Moldova (2012) and Similar Cases
Significance: Continues the M.C. line - State must effectively investigate sexual offences. Failure of
investigation engages Convention liability. Trauma-informed practice and victim-centred approach support
compliance.

Part B - Irish Procedural Authorities Applied to Sexual Offences
B.1 The People (DPP) v Walsh [1980] IR 294 - Arrest Reasonable Suspicion
Operational Application: Every sexual offences arrest requires the arresting Garda's articulable reasonable
suspicion. Notebook capture of the basis. The basis is typically the complainant's account plus any
corroborating preliminary evidence.

B.2 The People (DPP) v Madden [1977] IR 336 - Voluntariness
Operational Application: Sexual offences suspects in custody are processed under standard custody
framework. Voluntariness is challenged at trial particularly where the suspect is under significant emotional
pressure (the suspect may himself be a victim of sexual offences history; or have other vulnerabilities). Medical
assessment at custody where appropriate; interview when fit.

B.3 The People (DPP) v Buck [2002] 2 IR 268 - Inferences
Operational Application: Inferences from silence may engage in sexual offences interviews - re. consent
claim, identity of complainant, presence at scene, communications evidence. Buck-strict compliance:
prescribed warning, consultation opportunity, statutory scope.

B.4 The People (AG) v Casey (No.2) [1963] IR 33 - Identification
Operational Application: Casey warning engages where the prosecution case depends on visual identification
- stranger rape, DFSA where complainant's memory of identification is compromised, cases where suspect
identification is disputed. VIPER, ADVOKATE, multiple-source identification support.

B.5 DPP v Gormley and White [2014] IESC 17
Operational Application: Sexual offences suspects' pre-interview solicitor consultation rights - facilitate
without delay. The case stakes intensify suspect engagement with legal advice.

B.6 DPP v Doyle [2017] IESC 1
Operational Application: Solicitor presence in interview where requested. Standard practice in serious sexual
offences cases.

B.7 DPP v JC [2015] IESC 31 - Modern Exclusionary Rule
Operational Application: Sexual offences cases are intensely contested on procedural grounds. JC margin is
narrow. Procedural rigour at every step - SATU evidence handling, chain of custody, custody compliance,
interview compliance, warrant compliance.

B.8 Damache v DPP [2012] IESC 11 - Warrant Independence
Operational Application: Search warrants for suspect premises / devices in sexual offences cases to District
Court for independent authorisation. CSAM cases especially device-focused; warrant framework operative.

Part D - Evidence Authorities Specific to Sexual Offences
D.1 Sexual History Evidence - s.3 Criminal Law (Rape) Act 1981
S.3 CL(R)A 1981 (as amended) restricts cross-examination of a complainant on their previous sexual history
without leave of the court. The doctrine:
Cross-examination on previous sexual experience with the accused - may be permitted in limited circumstances.
Cross-examination on previous sexual experience with persons other than the accused - generally prohibited
unless leave granted on specific grounds.
Leave granted only where exclusion would be unfair to the accused.
Application made in advance; complainant's interests considered.
Operational implication: the complainant's sexual history is not generally an evidential battleground; the
investigation focuses on the consent dynamics of the incident in question.

D.2 Recent Complaint Doctrine
In sexual offence cases, evidence of recent complaint by the complainant to a third party may be admissible to
rebut suggestions of recent fabrication and as part of the narrative. The doctrine has been substantially expanded
by modern Irish practice recognising that delayed complaint does not undermine credibility and that the
complaint may be admissible even if not strictly 'recent'.

D.3 Anonymity - Statutory Protections
s.7 Criminal Law (Rape) Act 1981 - complainant identifying information protection.
Breach of anonymity is an offence.
Anonymity continues even after trial conclusion.
Internal Garda case management should respect anonymity (need-to-know basis).

D.4 Video-Recorded Interview Evidence
Criminal Evidence Act 1992 permits video-recorded interviews of vulnerable witnesses (children, persons with
mental impairment) to be admitted as evidence-in-chief at trial. The witness must be available for crossexamination. Operational use: child complainants, complainants with disabilities, in some cases adult
complainants by special direction.

D.5 Special Measures at Trial
Beyond video-recorded evidence-in-chief, special measures available for vulnerable complainants at trial:
Screen between complainant and accused.
Live video link from a separate room.
Removal of wigs / gowns where appropriate.
Cleared courtroom where appropriate.
Use of intermediaries for complainants with communication needs.

Disclosure of complainant's counselling / therapy records is a recurrent issue in sexual offences trials. The

Counselling notes generally confidential.
Defence may apply for disclosure on specific grounds.
Court conducts balancing exercise.
Where disclosure ordered, may be limited / redacted.

Part E - Sentencing Authority in Sexual Offences
E.1 The People (DPP) v Tiernan [1988] IR 250
Citation: [1988] IR 250 (Supreme Court).
Facts: Tiernan was convicted of rape; sentence appealed by DPP for being unduly lenient. The Supreme Court
used the case to set out the structured discretion framework for sentencing.
Holding: Sentencing in rape (and across criminal law) is a matter of structured judicial discretion. The court
identifies the gravity of the offence, aggravating factors, mitigating factors, and arrives at a sentence
proportionate to the offence and the offender. The court rejected a tariff-based approach but indicated that rape
is a serious offence attracting substantial sentencing.
Significance: Foundational. Tiernan is the framework for all sentencing including sexual offences.
Operational Application: Sexual offences sentencing aggravators: vulnerability of complainant (age,
disability, intoxication); position of trust by accused; serious violence / weapons; sustained / repeated offending;
multiple complainants; recording / dissemination of material; impact on complainant; failure to plead.
Mitigators: plea (early); genuine cooperation; first offence; offender circumstances; absence of force beyond
the offence itself.

E.2 The People (DPP) v WD [2008] - and Modern Sentencing Cases
WD and subsequent Court of Criminal Appeal / Court of Appeal authority refines the Tiernan framework in
sexual offences. The structured analysis is now well-established. Indicative themes:
Aggravating features intensify sentence within the available range.
Plea attracts substantial discount where early and genuine.
Position of trust offences attract aggravation regardless of other factors.
Sustained pattern / multiple offences attract substantial sentencing.
