Part F - Key Operational Takeaways from Case Law
F.1 The M.C. v Bulgaria Operational Imperative
Consent-based investigation is the Convention standard. Absence of physical resistance does not negate nonconsent. The Garda investigation engages realistically with the dynamics of sexual violence including freezing,
submission, intoxication, coercion.

F.2 The O'Keeffe Institutional Dimension
The State has positive obligations in respect of child sexual abuse, particularly in institutional contexts. Children
First mandatory reporting and the wider child protection framework operate within this. Sexual offences
investigations involving institutional contexts engage the wider State-obligation framework.

F.3 Procedural Rigour
Sexual offences cases are contested intensely at trial. The JC margin is narrow. Procedural compliance at every
step is the foundation.

F.4 The 2017 Act Consent Framework
Active, voluntary, ongoing consent. The s.48 statutory indicators. The reasonableness limb of mistaken-belief
defence. The framework operates throughout investigation and informs case-build, interview strategy, and trial
presentation.

F.5 Anonymity
Statutory complainant anonymity respected internally and externally. Investigation discussion on need-to-know
basis.

F.6 Trauma-Informed Practice
Recognising trauma effects on memory, demeanour, narrative, disclosure pattern. Specialist Garda
interviewers; multi-session approach; victim-controlled pace.

F.7 The Sentencing File Build
Aggravating and mitigating factors documented; Victim Impact Statement supported; antecedent statement
accurate; the file supports informed Tiernan sentencing.

How to Use Volume 7
Volume 7 covers road traffic policing - the highest-volume operational area of Garda work. The Road Traffic
Act framework spans from minor fixed-charge offences through to fatal collision investigation. The structure
follows the Volumes 1-6 pattern:

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

The RTA Operational Framework
Road traffic policing operates at the intersection of high-volume routine enforcement and high-stakes serious
investigation. A single shift may include FCN issuance, vehicle stops, a roadside checkpoint, a drink-driving
arrest, and (occasionally) a fatal collision callout. The framework calibrates to:

The Type of Conduct
Minor offences (speeding, mobile phone, seatbelt) - FCN system primarily.
Document offences (licence, insurance, tax, NCT) - summons / FCN.
Impaired driving - testing procedure, arrest, evidential sampling.
Quality-of-driving offences (careless, dangerous) - investigation, summons / charge.
Fatal / serious injury collisions - full investigation framework with Garda Forensic Collision Investigation.

The Statutory Framework
Road Traffic Acts 1961-2024 (multiple amendments).
Road Traffic (No.2) Act 2011 and subsequent.
Specific frameworks for drink/drug driving (RTA 2010).
Penalty Points (RTA 2002).
Fixed Charge Notices (RTA 2002 / 2006).

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Garda Roads Policing Structure
Divisional Roads Policing Units - patrol, enforcement, RTC response, checkpoints.
Forensic Collision Investigation (FCI) - specialist investigation of fatal / serious-injury collisions.
National Roads Policing Bureau - intelligence, support, national operations.
Standard Garda members - the principal operational force; most road traffic engagement is by district members
on regular duty.

Volume 7 Core Mnemonics
BREATH - Preliminary Breath Test Procedure
B - Basis for requirement (reasonable suspicion / event-triggered / MAT checkpoint).
R - Read out the requirement clearly.
E - Explain consequence of refusal (offence).
A - Administer test correctly per device protocol.
T - Take result; record contemporaneously.
H - Handle next step (negative - release; positive - arrest / station).

CHECKPOINT - MAT Operation Standard
C - Command authorisation (Inspector-level for MAT under s.10 RTA 2010).
H - Hi-vis visible and lawful authorisation visible.
E - Establish stops randomly (per authorisation pattern).
C - Conduct each stop professionally and uniformly.
K - Keep records (vehicles stopped, time, location).
P - Preliminary breath / drug test where required.
O - Outcomes documented.
I - Identify any arrest follow-up.
N - Note any incidents during operation.
T - Termination authorised; debrief.

CARELESS - DANGEROUS Continuum
Careless (s.52) - falls below the standard of a competent and prudent driver.
Dangerous (s.51) - drove in a manner (including speed) which was, having regard to all the circumstances,
dangerous to the public.
Dangerous causing death/serious harm (s.53) - dangerous driving with consequence; indictable.

SCENE - Fatal Collision Initial Response
S - Safety (yours, others - protect scene from secondary collisions).
C - Casualties (medical priority; ambulance request; preservation of evidence in life-saving).
E - Examine and preserve scene; cordon; control.
N - Notify FCI; Sergeant; supervisors.
E - Evidence preservation (vehicles, debris, marks, witnesses).
Verification reminder
Road Traffic Acts have been amended frequently. The drink/drug driving framework (RTA 2010) and subsequent
amendments are the modern operational base. Verify current statutory text, Medical Bureau of Road Safety
(MBRS) procedures, and Garda Roads Policing Bureau directives before relying.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Garda Code of Ethics - Road Traffic Application
Road traffic policing engages the Code of Ethics in operationally distinct ways. The high-volume, highdiscretion environment, the testing-procedure rigour, and the fatal-collision investigation context all demand
specific ethics awareness.

Standard 1 - Duty
Road safety is a public health responsibility. Drink/drug-driving, dangerous driving, and the
unlicensed/uninsured driver represent ongoing risk to the public. The Garda duty to protect life manifests in
road traffic policing as proactive enforcement and effective collision investigation. Approximately 150-200
fatalities per year on Irish roads - the State's road safety strategy depends on Garda enforcement.

Standard 4 - Use of Force
Force in road traffic is occasionally necessary - in dealing with non-compliant drivers, in arrest of intoxicated
suspects, in vehicle stops where the driver resists. Standard force analysis applies. Pursuit framework engages
where the driver fails to stop - subject to specific operational policy on pursuit (proportionality, risk assessment,
command authorisation).

Standard 5 - Honesty and Integrity
Road traffic procedures - particularly the testing procedure for drink/drug driving - are technically detailed and
rigorously contested at trial. Honesty in the recording of testing steps, calibration, sample handling, and timing
is the foundation of the case. The Avadenei jurisprudence emphasises strict compliance; honesty in
documenting compliance (or any deviation) is the operational standard.

Standard 6 - Impartiality
Road traffic enforcement engages with the entire driving public. Standard 6 requires impartial application same threshold of stop, breath test, FCN issuance across demographics and contexts. Targeting based on vehicle
type, driver appearance, or socioeconomic indicators is a Standard 6 trip-wire. The MAT (Mandatory Alcohol
Testing) framework supports impartiality - the random nature of stops removes individual selection bias.

Standard 7 - Speaking Up
Where colleagues engage in conduct that compromises road traffic integrity - selective enforcement, procedural
shortcuts, inappropriate discretion - the duty to speak up applies. Internal channels are available.

Discretion in Road Traffic Enforcement
Road traffic policing involves significant routine discretion. A speeding offence may be FCN, warning, or
summons; a minor mechanical defect may be advised or notified. Discretion must be:
Lawful - within the statutory framework.
Reasoned - based on identifiable factors.
Consistent - same threshold across cases.
Documented - the basis for the discretionary decision recorded.
Free of improper factors - the personal characteristics of the driver should not drive the discretion.

The Six Road Traffic Ethics Trip-Wires

Fatal Collision Investigation Integrity
Fatal collisions are the high-stakes end of road traffic policing. The investigation supports:

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

The criminal investigation (s.53 RTA 1961 or other charges).
The Coroner's inquest.
The family of the deceased.
The broader road safety learning framework.
Integrity of scene preservation, evidence handling, witness engagement, and reporting is not optional - it is
what makes the multiple legitimate processes work. Forensic Collision Investigators are engaged in major
incidents.

Compliments - Road Traffic Categories
Effective MAT operation - well-run checkpoint with proper procedure and clean evidential outcomes.
Strong drink/drug-driving case-build - testing procedure compliant, evidence intact, prosecution sustained.
Major fatal collision investigation - sustained engagement supporting the family, the prosecution, and the
Coroner.
Lifesaving intervention at collision scene.
Pursuit decision-making - the difficult judgment to discontinue or continue based on proportionate risk.
Sustained enforcement work that demonstrably reduces local road traffic risk.

The Welfare Dimension
Road traffic policing carries occupational cost - particularly the cumulative exposure to fatal and serious-injury
collisions. Family liaison work in fatal cases is emotionally demanding. Use Garda welfare services. Specialist
Forensic Collision Investigators have particular welfare considerations. Acknowledging the cost is professional.
